notes.anchor: the prospect, by no means in doubt and now plainly settled, of your triumph
notes.note: The triumph was the supreme honour for a victorious commander, a ceremonial procession through Rome voted by the Senate; Appius was angling for one on his return from Cilicia.
Cicero disowns a self-interested motive as "Epicurean," alluding to the Epicurean school's reputation for grounding action in one's own pleasure or advantage.
Pompey's nickname (longum subsellium) for the slow, crowded board of senators that adjudicated triumph-claims; the joke is that getting a triumph approved meant waiting on a long bench of colleagues.

notes.anchor: the knowledge itself of augural law
notes.note: Cicero had been co-opted into the college of augurs (in 53/52 BC, into the place left by the dead Crassus); Appius, a noted authority on augural lore, had promised him instruction in the discipline (ius augurium).
A supplicatio was a public thanksgiving voted by the Senate for a military success, with days of prayer at the temples; it was the usual prelude to a triumph, which Cicero is here soliciting.
The acclamation imperator by one's own troops after a victory was the formal prerequisite for seeking a supplicatio and triumph; Cicero won it in his Amanus campaign of 51 BC.
